retrieved_docs.content: PPCM results in a significant financial strain on healthcare resources due to the increased disability-adjusted life years, especially in low- and middle-income countries. To reduce disparities and mortality, resources may need to be directed to socially vulnerable communities.

retrieved_docs.title: Perinatal Depression -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Perinatal depression is a prevalent and potentially severe mood disorder that affects approximately 1 in 7 people during pregnancy or within the first year after childbirth. Perinatal depression stems from a combination of hormonal changes, genetic predisposition, and environmental factors, yet up to 50% of cases remain undiagnosed due to the stigma surrounding the condition and patients' reluctance to disclose symptoms. Unlike the transient "postpartum blues," perinatal depression is more severe, often manifesting as persistent sadness, low self-esteem, sleep disturbances, anxiety, and difficulties bonding with the baby. Effective recognition and management of perinatal depression are essential for optimizing the health outcomes of the parent and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Care of the New Mother -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: According to ACOG, at least 40% of women do not seek postpartum care. Several factors contribute to this trend, such as cultural differences, lack of insurance, lack of adequate family support, low socioeconomic status, poor anticipatory guidance, race, lack of good transitional care management, and poor access to home visits. According to the Pregnancy Mortality Surveillance System, non-Hispanic blacks have the highest maternal mortality. [24] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Brexanolone -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum depression presents as one of the most common complications after childbirth and can be a significant cause of suffering for the mother, infant, siblings, and family. [3] In the USA, the estimated prevalence of postpartum depression varies in states ranging from 8 to 20%, with an overall mean prevalence of 11.5%. Postpartum depression affects at a multifactorial level resulting in maternal suicide, inability to work, child morbidity associated with impaired mother-infant bonding, and malnutrition within the first year of life. [4] [14] [15] The long-term consequence of this disruptive parenting results in multiple adverse child outcomes, including increased risk for behavioral problems, lower mathematics grades, and development of depression. [16] Therefore screening and early treatment for postpartum depression are essential. Many women treated with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) for postpartum depression do not achieve adequate response or full remission of depressive symptoms. [17] [18] Therefore, an urgent need for therapy specifically designed for postpartum depression was needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Blues(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Like all psychiatric disorders, the prevention of postpartum blues would involve mediation of the major risk factors. Unlike most other psychiatric disorders, however, most of the risk factors for postpartum blues – such as parity, delivery status, history of depression or bipolar disorder, or history of menstrual-related mood changes – are nonmodifiable. However, the most important factor in managing postpartum blues is the early identification of symptoms, prompt intervention, facilitation of open discussions with patients and their families, and access to mental health resources and professionals. It is also important that patients are screened for suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation towards the infant, paranoia, or psychotic features. It is also essential if symptoms were to develop and be identified that resources are in place to ensure that new mothers feel adequately supported.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Care of the New Mother -- Function -- Components of Postpartum Care
retrieved_docs.content: Nutrition and exercise: Women at higher risk for postpartum weight retention are those with higher gestational weight gain, black race, and lower socioeconomic status, which at the same time increase their risk of future obesity and type 2 diabetes. [10] Advise women to adopt a variety of healthy, balanced diets and resume their normal dietary habits. All breastfeeding mothers need to take an extra 500 calories per day. Avoid strenuous activities in the early postpartum period, and take plenty of rest for the first 2-3 weeks. slowly start with non-impact activities such as walking, and a gradual return to previous activities is recommended. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Infection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum infections represent a critical yet often underrecognized threat to maternal health, contributing significantly to global morbidity and mortality. Affecting 5% to 7% of postpartum patients, these infections account for up to 15% of deaths following delivery and rank among the top causes of peripartum mortality in both low- and high-resource settings. Delays in recognition and management contribute to poor outcomes, with a majority of sepsis-related deaths considered preventable.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Blues(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum “blues,” though not formally recognized as a diagnosis by the DSM-5, is a phenomenon that is extremely prevalent in the immediate time period after delivery. As postpartum blues are, by definition, mild, transient, and self-limited, they do not cause significant distress or disability in an individual’s life. However, the need for prompt identification and intervention, namely the access to social support systems and other mental health resources like counseling, is underscored by the significant risk of developing postpartum depression or psychosis in those with postpartum blues.

retrieved_docs.title: Perinatal Depression -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: During pregnancy, many patients who are at risk of developing perinatal depression can be identified. These patients, along with their families, should be provided with information and education regarding perinatal depression. The information should be reinforced during postpartum hospitalization and after discharge. [8] Childbirth education classes teach new parents to seek help and the support that they might need for childbirth. By teaching patients and their partners about the signs and symptoms of perinatal depression, educators can increase the chance that a patient with this condition receives proper treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Care of the New Mother -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The postpartum period begins soon after the baby's delivery usually lasts 6 to 8 weeks, and ends when the mother’s body has nearly returned to its pre-pregnant state. The postpartum period is important for both short-term and long-term health and well-being for a woman and her newborn. This activity should help the interprofessional team provide comprehensive postpartum care for the new mother.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Birth After Cesarean Delivery -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The degree of social support and financial stability also seemed to influence women's decisions. Women who were married or perceived themselves as having a strong social support system were more likely to prefer a subsequent section, likely due to increased recovery time postsurgery and the greater need for help during this time. Alternatively, women who had difficulty with recovery or complications during their initial surgery were far less likely to opt for another cesarean section. Cultural components seem to play a role in preference for VBAC, evident in the variability of preference based on ethnicity; "nearly 75% of Black women reported preferring a vaginal birth for their next delivery, compared to 54% of Latina women and 43% of White women." Women who desired to have 3 or more future children strongly favored VBAC, seeing as recurrent cesarean sections can increase risk with each subsequent surgery. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Psychosis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Like any other mental illness, postpartum psychosis not only affects the mother and the infant but has an equal impact on families and caregivers. It is crucial for the treatment team to be able to understand the magnitude of physical and emotional stress the partner and other family members are going through and address all their questions and concerns in an empathetic manner. [31] Patients should be screened for signs of mental illness during pregnancy and after childbirth. Women planning to get pregnant, who are predisposed to developing postpartum psychosis should be counseled and informed about the illness course and outcomes and the risks associated with the disease and treatment options available so they can arrive at an informed decision.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: Amniotic cavity and membrane problem, antepartum or intrapartum hemorrhage associated with leiomyoma, and emotional or mental disease in the mother that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are all classified as maternal conditions during pregnancy. The amniotic cavity and membrane problem represents a structural obstetric complication that can affect the integrity of the gestational sac and fetal environment. Hemorrhage occurring before or during labor that is linked to leiomyoma (uterine fibroids) reflects a vascular complication that may lead to significant blood loss and impact both maternal and fetal outcomes. Emotional or mental disease in the mother, such as depression, anxiety, or other psychiatric disorders, constitutes a psychological condition that can interfere with prenatal care, labor progress, and postpartum recovery. Recognizing these diverse maternal conditions—structural, hemorrhagic, and mental health–related—is essential for comprehensive prenatal assessment, risk stratification, and the implementation of targeted interventions to improve maternal and neonatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: Gestation abnormality and pregnancy and puerperal disorders are both linked to the condition ولادة قبل الأوان (preterm birth), indicating that preterm birth is classified as a specific type of gestation abnormality and also falls within the broader category of pregnancy and puerperal disorders. This dual classification underscores that preterm birth represents both an abnormality of the gestational process and a disorder affecting the pregnancy‑postpartum continuum, which is important for clinicians to recognize when assessing risk factors, planning management, and providing comprehensive care to patients experiencing or at risk for early delivery.

retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
retrieved_docs.content: Antepartum or intrapartum hemorrhage associated with leiomyoma and emotional or mental disease in the mother that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium are both categorized as maternal conditions during pregnancy. The hemorrhagic condition reflects a bleeding risk linked to uterine fibroids (leiomyoma) that can occur before or during labor, while the emotional or mental disease denotes psychiatric or psychological disorders in the mother that adversely affect the gestational, delivery, or postpartum periods. Recognizing both types of maternal conditions is essential for comprehensive prenatal care, as they can influence maternal health, fetal development, and overall pregnancy outcomes, necessitating appropriate obstetric management of leiomyoma‑related bleeding and timely mental‑health assessment and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium” encompasses a range of adverse conditions that can arise during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period, including the specific complication “Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” which is recognized as a distinct type of obstetric complication. Within this broader group, the subcategory “COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY” is positioned as a child of the overall complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that it focuses on adverse events that are primarily associated with the gestational phase itself. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of obstetric complications, linking general categories to more specific clinical presentations that are important for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in maternal health care.
